data.title: SoFi: Execution Makes It A Buy Again (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
SoFi (SOFI) released full-year 2025 results recently. I revisited this stock to catch up and because I've seen a lot of discussion about the quality of its loans. I felt more positive, though, so I am here to upgrade.

Summary of Previous Theses

I originally covered SOFI in July 2024. It was ranging from $6 to $7 per share back then. This was a lot lower than the period after its IPO.

Looking at the chart above, it had fallen from highs in the $20s. I thought things were going well, however. I mean, they finally had profitable quarters.

In 2022, they acquired Golden Pacific, which got them a bank charter. I was skeptical at first, but I was proved wrong. The $22M acquisition had created $500M in savings on interest expense because they could use deposits instead. I thought earnings could reach $1.5B in five to ten years. I also thought market cap expansion would pull the share price up to $12-$15. So I rated it a buy.

I liked a lot of what SoFi was doing. It was creating more sources of revenue. It wasn't depending on student loans as much. It created a burgeoning tech platform that actually partners with other financial institutions.

BUT, stock was at $17 then, and I felt it had caught up to my valuation. Because of that, I changed my mind and re-rated it Hold.

Full-Year 2025 Results

Quite a bit of time has passed since I covered SOFI, and the financial results show that a lot has changed since 2024.

Net revenue grew from $2,675M in 2024 to $3,613M in 2025. This was a consequence of gains in both net interest income and noninterest income. This is important, given SoFi's emphasis on their dual nature of banking/lending and serving as a tech platform for financial partners. On that note, let's consider revenue when broken down among their three reportable segments:

  • Lending
  • Technology Platform
  • Financial Services

All segments enjoyed growth, but financial services was the fastest ($821M to $1,542M), and it demonstrated a very strong contribution profit margin.

The Financial Services segment leverages the flexibility of their mobile-first business model, creating many tuck-in products that go beyond their initial loan product (historically, student loans). These include personal loans and lines of credit, yes, but it has expanded to include investing, payments, and more recently crypto services.

The Technology Platform segment, effectively, leverages SoFi's infrastructure into a product that SoFi can contract out to their partners. While it makes sense that they would grow their in-house services more, this is a nice way to capture extra revenue.

When looking at other metrics that indicate the size of SoFi's business, the higher revenue across these segments is unsurprising. Total members grew from 10.1M in 2024 to 13.7M in 2025. The number of the financial products in use also grew from 14.7M to 20.2M in that period.

In spite of growth on the top line, SoFi reported a slight decline in net income: from $498.7M in 2024 to $481.3M in 2025. While noninterest expense grew about $600M, this was sufficiently covered by the $1B gain in net revenue. Rather, the biggest impact was that 2024 included a net tax benefit, while they incurred taxes in 2025. Income before taxes grew from $233.3M to $525.9M, indicating that the underlying earnings power grew substantially.

The balance sheet mostly showed the growth as its banking and lending are concerned. Total deposits grew by about $11.2B, supporting a $10.5B increase in total loans on the balance sheet.

This helped to reduce their debt from $3.1B to $1.8B and to grow their cash balance. This occurred in late December, at $27.50 per share. SoFi took advantage of the higher valuation, rather than being forced to issue equity like a distressed business.

In summary, it was a very good year for SoFi, and the various, moving pieces that I discussed reflect how it is maturing and executing on its vision of being a more well-rounded fintech.

Outlook and Valuation

Let's consider a few numbers. In 2024, I opined that SoFi could reach $1.5B in earnings in five to ten years. At that time, earnings were annualizing to about $400M. 2026's guidance of adjusted net income, per the latest earnings call, was $825M. This is showing tremendous progress on their execution. Not only that, management guided a net revenue CAGR of 30% from 2025 to 2028, with an EPS CAGR of 38% to 42%.

That suggests that SoFi can hit that $1.5B milestone. It's optimistic guidance, so we should assess the growth drivers first.

The first one to assess is their crypto product. As that was introduced in 2025, I believe we will see it use cases develop in the coming years, particularly now that the GENIUS Act is passed and the consequences this has for banks. As CEO Anthony Noto explained in the earnings call:

Since March, when the OCC made crypto permissible for national banks, we've moved with urgency to bring new products to our members. In October, we enhanced our unprecedented money movement offering with the launch of SoFi Pay, our first payment product that leverages blockchain technology to provide fast, seamless, low cost and safe international payments.

I believe SoFi's tech-friendly, innovative roots position it well to utilize crypto and blockchain technology, not simply as a retail gimmick but for actual use cases that create value for members.

Other new services that were launched in 2025 (such as an AI coach and international remittances) will be important to watch. For SoFi to maintain such high growth, they will need to increase revenue per member, while also continuing to gain members. Adoption of the newer products in the upcoming quarters will give hints of that.

Given their swift execution and adaptation in the last two years, I think the next goal to consider is if SoFi can do $3B in earnings in five to ten years. If they continue to perform as guided, they will hit this. SoFi is currently a $21.3B market cap near $16.50 per share. If they simply maintain a P/E of 20, that's a near-triple. In short, I believe SoFi can reach $45 per share.

Risk Factors

Initially, some of my concern was their ability to compete effectively with other banks and financial institutions. The last two years show that they compete very well, even being something of a pioneer. Rather, I think the main risks come back to the underwriting portion of the revenue, which somewhat explains why they've diversified into more fee-based revenues.

While personal loans have grown and now make up a majority of the loan book, student loans still accounted for 36% of their $35.8B in loans. It's a peculiar balance sheet for this reason. Student loans are, by their nature, unsecured. Young people don't have a lot of assets, and lenders can't just repossess a degree.

Most of the personal loans are unsecured too, but there are some key differences from the student product. Personal has smaller balances, shorter durations, and higher average coupons (13.11% vs. students' 5.87%). While the default rate is higher, it isn't significantly higher and is adequately addressed by the higher coupon.

Getting to the point, SoFi was formed in 2011, after the 2008 financial crisis. Its core product has never been subject to a similar stress test as that of subprime mortgages (which even had the benefit of collateral). If the stress test happens, a lot of these student loans can quickly become worthless.

Personal loans have this same problem, really. SoFi can tighten credit and let loans run off in preparation for a distress, but mortgages or secured business loans are not present.

Conclusion

SoFi continues to innovate and win business in what is a competitive space. 2025 showed their ability to integrate new products and earn their relevance. I have come to view SoFi as something of a universal fintech, similar to how I view Microsoft (MSFT) in comparison to other Mag7 stocks.

The main thing that could hold them back is that (mobile-friendly as they are) they don't have a lot of collateral to collect if loans default. One can doubt the margin of safety being sufficient as this stock rises into the $20s, but as it's well below that, I upgrade to Buy in recognition of their continually improving business.

data.title: Columbia Select Mid Cap Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

  • Institutional Class shares of Columbia Select Mid Cap Growth Fund returned -3.51% for the period ending December 31, 2025.
  • The fund's benchmark, the Russell Midcap Growth Index, returned -3.70% during the same period.
  • The fund performed essentially in line with the Russell Mid Cap Growth Index for the quarter. Stock selection within information technology and health care, along with an overweight to materials, led positive contributions to relative performance, while selection within financials, industrials and consumer discretionary weighed most heavily on returns.

Market overview

The tepid returns for mid-cap equities came despite better-than-expected economic growth, robust corporate earnings results, and inflation that largely remained below an annualized rate of 3%. The U.S. Federal Reserve continued to ease policy, cutting rates by a quarter point at its meetings in October and December and announcing an end to the multi-year effort to reduce the size of its balance sheet. Stocks also remained supported by ongoing excitement surrounding the artificial intelligence (AI) theme, albeit with a brief stretch of concern in November that AI-related equities were in a "bubble."

Within the mid-cap universe, growth and value stocks returned -3.70% and 1.42%, as gauged by the Russell Midcap Growth and Russell Midcap Value indices, respectively.

Top holdings (% of net assets): as of December 31, 2025

Take-Two Interactive Software (TTWO) 3.06
Carpenter Technology (CRS) 2.95
Howmet Aerospace (HWM) 2.89
Mongodb (MDB) 2.85
Spotify Technology (SPOT) 2.76

DraftKings-CI A (DKNG) 2.29
Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security.

Quarterly portfolio recap

Contributors to relative performance

Carpenter Technology is a metallurgical company that produces superalloys used in the “hot” sections of aircraft engines. The stock advanced on record quarterly profitability, strong free-cash-flow generation and confident fiscal year 2026 guidance, supported by robust demand within the aerospace and power-generation markets. Carpenter posted earnings beats and raised guidance throughout 2025, which was the most profitable year in its history. The company remains positioned to benefit from tight specialty metal alloy markets, which have led to pricing strength and structurally higher margins. These tailwinds stand to intensify in 2026, as Boeing begins to ramp up production of commercial airplanes.

MongoDB provides an open-source Not Only SQL (NoSQL) document database that stores data in document form within collections, rather than in traditional tables composed of columns and rows. The stock came under pressure in early 2025 on concerns around perceived competition within the AI space. However, MongoDB’s shares moved sharply higher over the last few months of the year, as it became clear that the flexibility offered by its product is relevant to AI applications, which rely on unstructured real-world data as opposed to information presented in tables; this drove strong sales of MongoDB’s Atlas cloud offering.

Sandisk is a leading supplier of flash memory storage products including solid-state drives (SSDs). Historically, hard disk drives (HDDs) have been the preferred storage solution within data centers, including AI data centers. However, with the increased demand from AI data centers, HDDs are essentially out of stock and their prices have skyrocketed. As a result, the AI data center market has pivoted to SSDs, driving their prices up as well and creating a new market opportunity for Sandisk.

Detractors from relative performance

AeroVironment (AVAV) was historically a supplier of drones, but underwent a radical transformation with the acquisition of Blue Halo, expanding its product offerings to include satellite communications, high-powered lasers and space technologies. The stock rose sharply for much of 2025 on exceptionally strong results. However, AeroVironment shares moved steadily lower beginning in mid-October, as it became clear that the U.S. government shutdown would significantly impact the timing of various contracts. We maintain a positive view on the stock, as AeroVironment is well positioned to benefit from rising global demand for drones and remote warfare solutions.

Spotify Technology is a leading music streaming service that has expanded its product offerings to include audiobooks and video podcasts, while growing its subscriber base and demonstrating strong pricing power. After a run of strong performance, the stock has come under pressure from profit-taking. We maintain a positive view on Spotify, given its resilient subscription model that should largely insulate the company from weaker consumer sentiment in a potential economic downturn.

Targa Resources (TRGP) is a midstream pipeline company focused on natural-gas liquids. The company delivered record earnings and strong capital returns in 2025, benefiting from its pipeline exposures to the Permian Basin in western Texas, which has led U.S. onshore production. The stock has stalled in recent quarters as growth expectations for the company normalized. In addition, investors began to take profits on Targa as the supply of oil and associated natural gas has increased relative to demand, leading to an easing in drilling activity.

Outlook

While we maintain a strong focus on individual stock selection, we of course monitor the macroeconomic backdrop, with an eye toward the potential impact on the companies in which we invest. In this vein, we see some potential tailwinds for company performance and investor risk sentiment in the coming year. Inflation has been more contained than originally feared in some quarters. In addition, some provisions in the tax and budget bill signed in July should provide a boost to consumer sentiment, and while there have been some signs of softening employment on the margins, layoffs have been measured and contained, while wage growth has remained reasonable. While advances from the ongoing investment in AI will take some time to spread throughout the economy, we expect the ultimate productivity enhancements to enable a higher rate of non-inflationary growth, which could allow the Fed to maintain rates at relatively low levels by historical standards.

We view the environment heading into 2026 as supportive of our focus on adding value by identifying the best growth opportunities in the mid-cap space. The core tenet of our process is to focus on identifying innovators and durable business models, products and services. More broadly, we continue to look for companies in growing markets that take market share as they generate high or rising returns on investment. Such companies will grow earnings and cash flow faster than revenue. Our intrinsic value approach helps us determine which stocks to choose for total opportunity and risk/reward. We are finding numerous opportunities as innovation drives changes, and well-positioned companies can expand profits and ultimately free cash flow. Many of these mid-cap growth stocks are attractively valued relative to their large-cap counterparts and have been overlooked by investors for some time.

data.title: Fidelity Select Materials Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
FUND INFORMATION

Manager(s): Ashley Fernandes

Trading Symbol: FSDPX

Start Date: September 29, 1986

Size (in millions): $699.06

Morningstar Category: Fund Natural Resources

Stock markets, especially foreign markets, are volatile and can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market, or economic developments. The materials industries can be significantly affected by the level and volatility of commodity prices, the exchange value of the dollar, import and export controls, worldwide competition, liability for environmental damage, depletion of resources, and mandated expenditures for safety and pollution control. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks. The fund may have additional volatility because of its narrow concentration in a specific industry. Non-diversified funds that focus on a relatively small number of stocks tend to be more volatile than diversified funds and the market as a whole.

Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Select Materials Portfolio (FSDPX) is a sector-based equity-focused strategy that seeks to outperform its benchmark through active management.
  • We conduct in-depth fundamental research, supported by Fidelity's deep and experienced global cyclical team, to develop a differentiated view from consensus on product- and end-market trends.
  • We follow a contrarian investment approach and focus on companies in which we anticipate a positive change in business fundamentals before it is recognized and properly valued in the company's stock price.
  • We also look for reasonably valued stocks of firms with improving returns on capital, healthy free-cash-flow yields, the potential for price-earnings multiple expansion, or defensive-growth characteristics.
  • Sector strategies could be used by investors as alternatives to individual stocks for either tactical- or strategic-allocation purposes.

For definitions and other important information, please see the Definitions and Important Information section of this Fund Review.

Market Review

For the three months ending December 31, the materials sector, as measured by the MSCI U.S. IMI Materials 25/50 Linked Index, gained 1.70%, while the broad-market S&P 500® index advanced 2.66%.

Stocks entered October on an uptrend, but with the federal government in a shutdown that would last until November 12. On October 29, the central bank lowered its benchmark federal funds rate by 0.25 percentage points at its second consecutive meeting, as it looked to balance a recent slowdown in hiring and inflation running higher than its 2% target. In November, stocks weathered the ongoing shutdown and a brief mid-month sell-off of mega-cap growth stocks that mostly reflected concern about an AI bubble. However, the focus quickly returned to a U.S. economy largely driven by massive AI-related outlays by big tech. The Fed then cut rates by another quarter point on December 10.

Against this backdrop, results for the materials sector were decidedly mixed. By a wide margin, the most significant gains were among metals segments, both industrial and precious. Gold stocks (+16%) continued their remarkable run, with the price of the precious metal hitting multiple record highs during the quarter. Copper (+30%) surged, as high demand and tightening supply contributed to sharply higher prices for the commodity, while steel (+17%) and aluminum (+52%) each rose as well, with U.S.-based producers helped by tariff policies and improving demand. Though a very small component of the sector index, silver (+59%) gained strongly, benefiting from rising industrial demand as well as increasing usage as a safe-haven asset, like gold.

In contrast, most chemicals segments were notably weak, hurt by declining industrial activity, which led to falling prices and lower margins for many firms. The largest industry group in the MSCI index, specialty chemicals, gained about 2%, while the second-biggest, industrial gases, returned -10%. Together, those two segments represented roughly 42% of the index during the quarter. Fertilizers and agricultural chemicals (-10%) and commodity chemicals (-7%) struggled as well. Lastly, construction materials – one of the larger segments, at about 13% of index assets – finished essentially break-even (0%). ■

Performance Review

For the quarter, the fund's Retail Class shares returned -0.43%, trailing the 1.70% advance of the MSCI U.S. IMI Materials 25/50 Linked Index, as well as the 2.66% gain of the broad-based S&P 500® index.

The fund's underperformance of the sector index was due primarily to stock selection, while industry selection modestly detracted. Specifically, picks and an overweight in fertilizers & agricultural chemicals hurt most, followed by investment choices in specialty chemicals.

An outsized stake in Mosaic, one of the world's largest producers of phosphate and potash fertilizers, returned roughly -30% the past three months and was the biggest individual relative detractor. Global prices for phosphate and potash dropped significantly in the second half of 2025 as supply normalized and demand softened. Lower selling prices for fertilizer weighed on Mosaic's revenue and margin; in its Q3 earnings update, the company reported revenue of $3.45 billion, lagging the consensus estimate. At year-end, the stock was our seventh-largest holding and second-biggest overweight.

A lighter-than-index holding in Freeport-McMoRan (+29%), a top global copper-mining firm and also a major gold producer, hurt as well. We underweighted the stock based on concerns about the company's geopolitical risk profile, but the fund maintained an overweight in the copper industry, which was beneficial this quarter.

Conversely, a sizable out-of-index position in Canada-headquartered copper producer First Quantum Minerals (+18%) was the top contributor to the fund's relative performance. The company has continued to regain its footing since Panama's government shut down its top copper-mining operation in late 2023. It's been helped by higher copper prices, as well as by high prices for gold, which the company mines at its site in Zambia.

Lastly, the portfolio's relative performance was aided by an overweight in aluminum producer Alcoa (+62%), which in October posted strong operational and financial performance for Q3, benefiting from higher pricing for the metal. We increased the holding. ■

Outlook and Positioning

As 2026 begins, the global and U.S. business cycles remain constructive, with monetary and fiscal easing in the U.S. likely to continue, in our view. Noteworthy trends in the U.S. include strong corporate fundamentals and earnings momentum, softening labor markets, and wealth-powered high-end consumption. Capital spending by corporations has experienced a powerful AI-driven upswing, although signs of broadening activity remain faint.

Made up of highly capital-intensive industries, materials is among the most cyclical and rate-sensitive of sectors. Policy interest-rate reductions by the U.S. Federal Reserve in October and December, and a weakening U.S. dollar, generally supported sector stocks the past three months – though, in our view, corporate fundamentals and supply-and-demand factors were the main forces behind investment outcomes for materials stocks this quarter. Going forward, we will continue to emphasize the industries we think have the strongest supply-demand profiles.

The two largest industry groups in the MSCI sector index are specialty chemicals, which on average held a roughly 23% weight within the index the past three months, and industrial gases, at about 19% of total index assets on average. At year-end, the fund was roughly neutral versus the index in industrial gases, while the specialty chemicals segment was the largest underweight.

In our estimation, companies in the industrial gases group have an attractive mix of defensive qualities – with steady, reliable earnings growth and pricing power – and the ability to participate in an economic recovery. That said, within the fund, the group was made up of just two large stock holdings: U.S.-based multinational firm Air Products and Chemicals, and U.K.-headquartered industry giant Linde. The latter is by far the biggest position in both the fund and the sector index. We trimmed the stake in Linde this quarter, and the position was roughly equal with the index's at year-end.

The biggest stakes within the specialty chemicals group were water treatment company Ecolab, refrigerant producer Solstice Advanced Materials – a new fund position this quarter – and paint and coatings firm Sherwin-Williams. Ecolab was the portfolio's second-largest holding and third-biggest overweight as of December 31.

Among smaller segments, the fund's largest industry overweight was in fertilizer & agricultural chemicals, though we reduced our position in the group this quarter. After experiencing price weakness in 2023 and 2024, global fertilizer prices rebounded in 2025, thanks to solid demand and tight supply of certain key chemicals. Major fund holdings here include Corteva, which specializes in seed and crop protection, and Mosaic. Though several fertilizer stocks, including Mosaic's, have retrenched in recent months after mixed earnings reports, we think overall fundamentals for the group are still improving.

The second-largest fund overweight was in copper. We like the long-term prospects for the metal, as it is a resource that is widely used in the production of electronics, electric vehicles and equipment that produces wind and solar power, and long-term supply appears to be constrained. Fund holdings here include First Quantum Minerals, which was the portfolio's top relative performer this quarter, as well as U.S. mining giant Freeport-McMoRan.

Over the past two years, gold has benefited from geopolitical uncertainty and its role as a store of value, given the fiscal challenges that many major Western economies are now facing. While we have substantial holdings in the segment, gold is a modest portfolio underweight at year-end, and we plan to be cautious with the stocks going forward, given the scale of recent growth.

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (less) than 1 is more (less) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

data.description:
Key takeaways

Fund performance

Invesco Short Duration High Yield Municipal Fund Class A shares outperformed its style-specific index, the Custom Short Duration High Yield Municipal Index.

Seeking attractive opportunities through collaborative management

Invesco Municipal Bond team uses a collaborative management approach. Relying on our size and experience, we seek to identify the best opportunities to achieve potentially better outcomes for shareholders.

Analysis focused on creditworthiness

Our team uses a bottom-up fundamental credit process focused on creditworthiness of individual issuers with an overlay of macroeconomic factors to capitalize on market inefficiencies. Our process has been time tested over full market cycles.

Manager perspective and outlook

• In the fourth quarter, investment grade, high yield and taxable municipals delivered positive returns of 1.42%, 1.11% and 1.05%, with annual returns of 4.25%, 2.46% and 7.89%, respectively. 1 Despite a lengthy federal government shutdown, long-duration municipal bonds performed well during the quarter, bolstering market strength. 1

• New municipal issuance reached $143 billion for the quarter and a record $584 billion for the year, surpassing last year's record $509 billion. Shifting interest rate policies, higher costs and political uncertainty likely encouraged more issuers to come to market. 1

• Net flows for municipal mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) were strong, totaling approximately $17.5 billion for the quarter and $52.4 billion for the year. 2

• The US Federal Reserve (Fed) cut the federal funds rate twice, by 0.25% in October and 0.25% in December. The Fed reiterated its commitment to balancing maximum employment and a 2% inflation target. 3

• We believe state and local municipal budgets remain healthy. While credit rating upgrades have moderated, upgrades outpaced downgrades in 2025, demonstrating to us strong fundamentals. 4

• Our experienced credit research team continues to seek out market dislocations, uncovering opportunities to add value for shareholders.

• Looking ahead, we see attractive opportunities in municipals. With prospects for more Fed interest rate cuts, steady issuance and ongoing demand for tax-exempt income, we believe high absolute yields and solid fundamentals make municipals a compelling choice.

Within the revenue bond segment, the fund's largest exposures are in the higher education, industrial development revenue/pollution control revenue (IDR/PCR) and hospital sectors.

Credit Conditions: We maintain a positive view on fundamentals in the municipal market, as credit rating upgrades have continued to outpace downgrades. While various sectors have come under pressure due to reduced federal support, inflation-driven cost pressures and negative political headlines, we continue to rely on our extensive bottom-up research capabilities to identify strong credits with attractive yields.

The fourth quarter began on a strong note despite record levels of new issuance and the longest federal government shutdown in history. Economic signals were mixed, with solid Gross Domestic Product (GDP) growth alongside persistent inflation and a weakening employment outlook. The Fed cut the federal funds target rate by 0.25% in both October and December, for a total of three rate cuts in 2025. Municipals rallied in October and delivered steady, positive returns through the end of the quarter. Yields on the short end of the municipal yield curve rose, with two- and five-year bond yields rising by 0.17% and 0.08%, respectively. 5 Overall, higher quality municipal credits generally outperformed during the quarter.

Contributors to performance

Contributors to relative return this quarter included the following:

Security selection in the IDR/PCR and local general obligation sectors added to relative performance during the quarter. Security selection in the tobacco sector and holdings in higher education also contributed to relative return. From a duration perspective, securities with durations between one and two years and between five and six years also contributed to relative performance.

Detractors from performance

Detractors from relative return this quarter included the following:

Security selection in the life care and transportation sectors detracted from relative performance. The fund's underweight in bonds with durations of 10 years and longer also detracted from relative return, as longer duration municipals rallied during the quarter.

data.title: Introducing NYSX: A Modern Tool For Evolving Markets
data.description:
Technology has undergone a profound shift over the past several decades. What was once a specialized industry focused on hardware and software has evolved into the connective infrastructure of the global economy. Today, technology capabilities power nearly every sector, blurring the lines between traditional industry boundaries. Yet many of the investment strategies used to access technology exposure were built for an earlier era. To address this disconnect, we are introducing the Global X NYSE® 100 ETF (NYSX)—a modern tool designed to capture true innovation.

Technology Is the Economy

Despite a proliferation of new and innovative investment solutions, the means of gaining exposure to broad-based growth and technology has been remarkably static for almost 30 years. The net result is increasingly large positions in funds that track indexes or sub-indexes that we believe decreasingly capture the nature of innovation and growth across the economy.

For many years, publicly traded technology companies were concentrated in particular exchanges, but today, development and deployment of new technology is a competitive imperative that touches just about every business. From financial technology firms that help companies reduce transaction costs to automated vehicles that help drive operational efficiency, the nature of technological leadership continues to evolve.

Current investment trends highlight the prominence of technology utilization across the broader economy. Information Processing Equipment and Software now account for more than 1/3 of all Private Nonresidential Fixed Investments, up from just 20% in 2007.1 Equipment and software producers are helping companies deploy new automation and digitalization technologies to stay on the leading edge of their respective industries. Technology leadership is not confined to a few companies or a single sector; it is one of the central organizing principles of the modern economy.

Limitation of Traditional Technology Proxies

Investors often turn to funds that track familiar benchmarks when seeking technology exposure, but as technology has expanded beyond traditional boundaries, common proxy approaches based on legacy indexes may no longer fully capture the leaders of the technology ecosystem.

Indexes based on a listing exchange have been a common means of seeking out technology-driven exposure. Yet we believe those indexes face two structural limitations. First, restricting eligibility to a single exchange can exclude technology leaders listed elsewhere. Historically, the requirements for listing on exchanges were different, and that drove corporate decision-making.2 Those distinctions are less relevant for companies going public today. While companies choose listing venues for a variety of structural and regulatory reasons, no major U.S. exchange holds a monopoly on next-generation technology.

Second, no major exchange is composed exclusively of technology-driven companies. Many exchange-based indexes are designed to measure the performance of that exchange or a subset of the exchange, such as the largest companies. As a result, exchange-based benchmarks can include businesses in traditional industries such as retail or consumer staples that may not align with a targeted technology allocation. The outcome can lead to exposure that is both incomplete and diluted for funds aiming to replicate these indexes.

Another common approach to isolating leading-edge companies is through indexes tracking the technology sector within a broader index. While intuitive, this framework relies on classification systems that were built for an earlier era. Consider the so-called "Magnificent 7" companies, often viewed as bellwethers of U.S. technology leadership. Despite this informal grouping, they span three GICS sectors and six industries by classification. Fewer than half are even formally categorized as technology companies.

A sector-based approach may appear to offer precision, but traditional classifications increasingly fail to reflect how next-generation technology now spans multiple sectors and underpins the broader economy.

Introducing the Global X NYSE® 100 ETF: A Modern Tool for Evolving Markets

The evolution of the digital ecosystem and its increasing penetration across the economy require new tools for investors. The Global X NYSE 100 ETF seeks to track the NYSE 100 Index, comprised of 100 leading companies listed on any major U.S. exchange.3 Rather than constraining the definition of technology to one sector, the NYSE 100 Index reflects the economic reality of innovation by expanding eligibility to select sub-industries within the Consumer Discretionary, Financials, Industrials, and Media & Communications sectors in addition to the entire Technology sector.

Expanding beyond traditional sector boundaries broadens the opportunity set, but without a thoughtful selection framework, size alone may still shape which companies rise to the top. Rather than relying solely on size to determine inclusion, the NYSE 100 Index applies a weighted scoring framework to identify the 100 highest-ranked companies that reflect the scale, liquidity, and growth characteristics of today’s expanding technology ecosystem.

  • Size (35%) - Full market capitalization across all listed and unlisted securities.
  • Liquidity (35%) - Three-month average daily traded value (ADTV).
  • Valuation (15%) - Trailing 12-month price-to-sales ratio.
  • Growth (15%) - Trailing 12-month net sales growth.

Once selected, constituents are weighted by modified market capitalization. This approach allows the index to retain the transparency of market cap weighting while applying a more intentional lens to identifying companies that have been shaping next-generation technology growth.

Why NYSX Matters Today

Technology’s expansion into a cross-sector ecosystem has become a defining feature of today’s market leadership. Many of the largest and most influential U.S. companies now operate as interconnected platforms spanning hardware, software, media, payments, mobility, and artificial intelligence. Their scale, profitability, and reinvestment capacity have positioned them at the center of equity market performance in recent years.

At the same time, the drivers of next-generation technology growth are broadening. Artificial intelligence infrastructure, cloud computing, digital advertising, e-commerce logistics, electronic payments, and connected mobility are increasingly interdependent. Leadership in one area often reinforces leadership in another, creating a circular ecosystem dynamic rather than isolated sector growth.

For investors, this environment presents a portfolio construction challenge. Traditional sector definitions may underrepresent key participants in this ecosystem, while investing in products that track broad market indexes may dilute exposure with companies less directly tied to technology-enabled growth. As technology continues to function as a foundational layer of the U.S. economy, a more intentional approach to capturing that ecosystem may be increasingly relevant.

The NYSE 100 Index that NYSX tracks was designed with this modern market structure in mind, as it aims to reflect where technology-driven growth is occurring today and how it may continue to evolve.

Conclusion

As technology becomes increasingly embedded across industries, investors may need tools that better reflect this evolving structure of the economy. Traditional approaches based on exchange listings or single sector classifications can struggle to capture the full breadth of companies driving technology-enabled growth. By tracking an index that expands the opportunity set across exchanges and sectors and applies a structured selection framework, the Global X NYSE® 100 ETF seeks to provide a modern approach to accessing the companies shaping today’s technology ecosystem.

Footnotes

  2. Bass, Berry & Sims. (September 29, 2025). Listing Decision: The New York Stock Exchange vs. the Nasdaq Stock Market.
  3. The NYSE 100 Index is designed to track the performance of 100 technology and tech-enabled growth companies that are listed on the New York Stock Exchange, NASDAQ, NYSE American, Cboe BZX or NYSE Arca.

data.title: AGI Bust, AI Boom: Why AI's Winners And Losers Aren't Who You Think
data.description:
Introduction

What AI future is the market currently pricing? Right now, trillions of dollars are being invested as though artificial general intelligence (AGI) is inevitable. Big companies are engaged in an expensive, frenzied arms race searching for this holy grail. But what if it doesn't exist?

In this article I make a three-part argument. First, there are strong reasons to doubt that AGI is achievable on current computing hardware. Second, that this entails diminishing returns on the trillions of dollars of capital being invested to create larger and larger models. And third, that the biggest beneficiaries from AI technologies are likely to be the companies that can best implement current AI tools to improve efficiency and productivity rather than the companies actually building the tools.

What Everyone Is Getting Wrong

Hyperscalers, AI Labs, and even governments have bought into a couple of false metaphors. First is the idea that the mind/brain is at all like a computer (and vice versa). Because we didn’t understand the mind, we analogized it to the most advanced thing we had created. It didn’t help that we named pieces of the computer after properties that our minds possess, like memory. In philosophy of mind, this false analogy birthed a theory called "Computationalism," which posits that consciousness is just the result of a complex information processing system. Therefore, any information processing system with requisite complexity could come to possess consciousness, self-awareness, and agency.

The second false metaphor is that neural networks are good models for how the brain works (and vice versa). Although neural nets have allowed us to build some tremendous algorithmic systems, they are digital simulations of how a brain physically works. A digital simulation of a hurricane can seem to behave just like a real hurricane, but it doesn't make your computer wet and your room windy. It lacks the physical properties required to give it real-world causal power. Connectionism, which is a brand of computationalism, is the philosophical inspiration for the development of artificial neural networks, which are the basis for nearly everything we call "AI" today.

At the moment, Computationalism/Connectionism is the implicit guarantor of trillions of dollars being spent in pursuit of AGI. Hyperscalers and AI labs are betting that the larger and more powerful they make the neural networks, the more intelligence will scale up. Just make data centers and training data sets bigger and bigger, and eventually AGI will emerge through some clever statistical tricks.

But the key differences between the brain and a computer give good reason to think this bet may be misplaced. The brain is a dynamic, integrated system. The brain remolds itself in response to your thoughts - synaptic weights are constantly in flux, while a computer is a fixed structure of dead silicon. A computer's physical data does change in response to software actions, but the structural architecture doesn't. Moreover, memory and processing are not separated in the brain as they are in computers. They exist in the same grey matter in a massively parallel, recursive feedback loop. A different philosophical theory called "Integrated Information Theory" (IIT) captures these key differences and provides a better account of what properties are likely important for conscious intelligence in my view. For the remainder of the article, I apply Integrated Information Theory to analyze current AI spending through a different lens than what is guiding current assumptions. Note that my thesis doesn't rest on the truth of IIT specifically. Whether IIT is correct or not, I think companies are likely throwing money into diminishing returns. IIT just provides a useful framework for this analysis.

The AI-Human Centaur Model

We’re likely decades or centuries away from creating a silicon-based competitor for a human brain. IIT would describe any apparent intelligence built on modern computer architecture as a “philosophical zombie,” completely void of subjective experience despite exhibiting outward signs of intelligence. AI would have the terminal disadvantage of having no ability to internally model the world like humans can. But does consciousness automatically equate to competence? Unfortunately for humans, no. We have our own shortcomings: our minds possess a litany of behavioral and cognitive biases that served as good heuristics for the vast majority of our evolution as a tribal hunter-gathering species. These heuristics become cognitive weaknesses when we use them to reason about money, relationships, politics, etc. So although AI has a fundamentally limiting weakness, so do humans.

In a 2005 Freestyle Chess tournament, a team of amateurs using personal computers beat grandmasters paired with supercomputers. Although chess engines have since become so advanced that human intervention no longer makes much difference, this event demonstrated something profoundly applicable to the advancement of AI today in my view. In the event, grandmasters paired with supercomputers had beaten grandmasters alone and supercomputers alone. But the entire tournament was won by amateurs utilizing multiple computers. It was the interactive system between the humans and computers that led to victory. The power of this combination became known as a “Centaur Model.” Computers supply the brute force of the lower half of the body, while humans supply the high-level, cognitive upper half.

Like modern chess, there may be domains in which humans add no value whatsoever (chess is fundamentally a mathematical, pattern-matching problem). But likewise, there will be domains where AI adds little advantage. And across most domains, in my view, human plus AI will be superior to one or the other exclusively. That is the nature of comparative advantage - each piece of the puzzle will concentrate in the places in which it is most economically productive. AI alone will do what AI alone is best at, humans alone will do what humans alone are best at, and AI/human hybrid centaurs will do what they are best at.

What Does This Mean For Investments?

The million-dollar question, of course, is how we should position our investments in response to this risk. I think there are a few major themes that are likely to emerge from the technological paradigm shift that AI represents:

1. AI is unlikely to replace all jobs

Jobs that are highly algorithmic are likely at risk, but jobs that require high-level reasoning and decision-making are likely protected (unless you want a “philosophical zombie” making important business decisions). Those who learn to utilize AI in a “Centaur Model” become far more valuable. Jobs in which accountability is important (which is actually most jobs) are also likely protected, because an AI lacking consciousness or awareness cannot reasonably be held accountable.

I am skeptical that we’ll allow philosophical zombies (AI agents) to run our businesses and economies. What could possibly go wrong? As Air Canada found out, it can whimsically change your company’s refund policy. Now the legal precedent is that you’re responsible for the information your AI agent communicates. When Reddit and Twitter threads are part of the training data of the LLM that underlies the “agent,” then I would be careful about putting such an agent in charge of anything too important. You might find that it’s simply echoing a Reddit complaint thread that was a part of its training data.

2. LLM progress likely plateaus

Some argue that LLM progress is actually increasing at a greater than exponential rate. LLMs like Claude are showing the ability to work for extended periods of time. Performance used to deteriorate over longer periods but is now being sustained. I think LLMs/AI can achieve near perfection in tasks where advanced pattern matching can create perfect results. LLMs are trained on a massive corpus of information, including previously programmed apps, so to create a new (basic) app is purely a pattern-matching problem. LLMs are interpolative - when a solution exists in their training data, then they can create a new solution by modifying a prior solution. But so far there is very little evidence of novel problem solving, i.e., extrapolative reasoning. Humans don’t just modify prior blueprints; we create new, novel blueprints. We might create a new programming language or new mathematics, or think of a new scientific theory, or just solve a problem in a way that we haven’t done before.

If I’m right, then companies are likely pouring hundreds of billions of dollars into bigger and bigger data centers and consuming massive amounts of our resources for only marginal gain. American and Chinese companies are both throwing as much money as they can toward “AGI,” hoping to win a winner-takes-all race. This does suggest that an “AI bubble” could be brewing. I would therefore advise investors to proceed with caution in taking any positions in which continual AI progress and spending are heavily baked into the price. If companies realize that they’ve already squeezed all the juice out of this particular architecture, the investment could quickly dry up.

3. Invest in companies that can benefit from implementing current AI

If you find yourself in agreement with what I’ve said so far, then the investment case becomes pretty clear in my view: short the AGI premium and invest in the “Centaur Adopters.” Think back to the last technological revolution in the early days of the internet: people assumed that the laws of financial physics had irreversibly changed. Gross margins, free cash flows, and ROIC were all relics of the “old economy,” while the new economy was based on clicks, eyeballs, and TAM. But ultimately companies were still subject to the old rules - they still had to turn a profit. Those who won leveraged the technology to improve their efficiency and productivity. Those who lost assumed that the new paradigm shift erased the importance of fundamentals.

In the internet boom, the market assumed that any fillintheblank.com was an automatic winner. The more the business thesis intersected with the technological revolution, the higher premium the market assigned to the company’s valuation. But really the opposite should have been the case. It was the peripheral companies tangentially deploying the technology to improve their productivity and efficiency that eventually won.

Carlota Perez outlines the distinct phases of technological revolutions in her work Technological Revolutions and Financial Capital: The Dynamics of Bubbles and Golden Ages. Although her work applies to very long-term technological revolutions - we would currently be in the tail end of the IT revolution in her mind - I believe the economic psychology she described is applicable to the current development of artificial intelligence. She describes four phases to “technological revolutions”:

Irruption Phase: The emergence of a revolutionary new technology. In the current iteration of this cycle, I would say the “Big Bang” moment was the release of ChatGPT on November 30th, 2022.

Frenzy Phase: Financial capital pours into the new technology at irrational levels, fueling a “bubble.” Financial capital and production capital are decoupled. In the Internet bubble, the frenzy was highlighted by AOL acquiring Time Warner, who had five times AOL’s revenues; Cisco’s value rising to 5.5% of US GDP; and Yahoo’s 1800 P/E ratio.

Synergy Phase: The bubble bursts, regulations get implemented, and financial/production capital are recoupled. In the internet cycle, this was the 2000s.

Maturity Phase: The market fully adapts to the new technology, investment opportunities dwindle, and the technology becomes standard infrastructure. This is where we are in the internet cycle. We no longer wonder how the internet will change the world; it’s just a normal part of our everyday lives now.

I created the graphic below to show how the advancement of AI fits in Perez’s framework:

I believe the market is overvaluing the “shiny toy” of AGI while undervaluing the efficiencies that legacy companies can squeeze out of current AI technologies. The sweet spot then is companies that can drive efficiency through AI adoption and implementation and where that margin expansion is not already priced in. The danger zone is the companies for which AGI success is more or less already priced in or businesses that provide boutique services that can be bypassed by "AI centaurs."

Note that my intention in this article is to help develop a mental model for how the economy will absorb a transformational technology. Nailing down which companies specifically are winners and losers in this broader worldview requires really careful, detailed consideration. These are my preliminary thoughts about which companies fit into the specific molds. Over time my worldview is unlikely to change, but my thoughts on how specific companies are likely to adapt and evolve could change. In future articles I'll do deeper dives into each of these companies specifically.

AI Centaur Opportunities

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A, BRK.B): The company is extremely decentralized - you’ve got the insurance businesses, logistics operations, energy companies, and others. The subsidiary managers have enormous autonomy, which means the entire organization can capture gains from AI efficiency organically, from a bottom-up rather than top-down approach. Moreover, all of the subsidiaries can greatly benefit from the implementation of the “AI Centaur” model (actuaries working for Geico and logistics managers for BNSF can both use AI tools to improve their productivity). They all involve pouring through numerical and textual data, which AI can help accelerate. I rate BRK.A and BRK.B as strong buys.

UnitedHealth Group (UNH): UnitedHealth is currently trading at about a 15x forward P/E ratio. UNH has, of course, had its troubles the last few years, but I think this company can greatly benefit from AI adoption. Healthcare bureaucracies have some of the longest runways for efficiency gains of any group of companies. Prior authorizations, claims processing, care management, and documentation can all incorporate current AI tools to make the processes far more efficient. AI diagnostic tools are also rapidly improving - better diagnostics can save the company money by catching problems early and therefore preventing costly interventions later on. The company currently trades at a discount to its sector averages as well as its own 5 year averages in nearly every category. I rate UNH a Strong Buy.

Since I started writing this article, UNH actually announced the incorporation of an "AI companion" named Avery to help customers and advocates with more personalized service. The agent can help clarify a member's coverage details and schedule appointments, along with a number of other useful functionalities.

Thomson Reuters (TRI): The centaur case for TRI is primarily its subsidiary, Westlaw, with its massive proprietary legal database. Because of AI’s propensity to hallucinate, legal briefs require human authorship. But the AI can still act as a rapid extraction engine over Westlaw’s database. AI can identify relevant case law and precedent in seconds, which drives massive efficiency without undermining the company’s business model. Although case law is publicly available, the graphic relational mappings between different cases that Westlaw has developed through its KeyCite system are not. Therefore, I rate TRI a “buy."

S&P Global/Moody’s (SPGI, MCO): These companies are massive proprietary data monopolies, and unlike TRI, their data isn’t publicly available. AI can multiply the efficiencies of analysts at these companies by processing far greater amounts of issuer data. Both companies have seen their stock prices take big hits recently due to AI-driven concerns, but I actually think the market has got this all wrong. The market is assuming “seat TAM shrinkage” for both companies (banks will purchase fewer analyst seats because LLMs make the banks’ own analysts more productive). But this is a classic Jevons paradox. If AI makes financial data analysis cheaper and faster, the consequence isn’t necessarily fewer seats. In fact, Jevons paradox would suggest that greater efficiency actually increases demand for the resource. Furthermore, the risk is specific to the Market Intelligence divisions of these companies, not to the core ratings business. Given both companies are trading at a discount, I rate them “buys.”

Danger Companies

My thesis entails that AI becomes commoditized and that LLM progress plateaus. It suggests that pouring trillions of dollars into data centers is only going to make our LLMs marginally better, not break through the ceiling to machine consciousness or AGI. This would lead to a deceleration in the infrastructure build out while simultaneously crushing the business models of companies whose products are commoditized by current AI.

Open AI/Anthropic/xAI: The frontier labs would likely be the biggest casualties from the failure to develop AGI. They’re spending money as though this is a guaranteed outcome in a winner-take-all game. In my thesis, LLMs become slightly differentiated commodities, but no one creates a conscious or self-aware entity. Without that breakthrough, I think capabilities will be more or less equal, and not enough to move from the Centaur AI model to true AGI.

LegalZoom (LZ): AI has already commoditized boilerplate legal forms and language. LegalZoom is a UI wrapper on top of boilerplate legal language. LLMs have now commoditized this specific service, which means that the company is really left with nothing proprietary. Unlike TRI, it doesn’t have a massive database of proprietary information that it can leverage AI across. AI simply eats the business model.

Upwork and Fiverr (UPWK, FVRR): These two companies act as cognitive middlemen for quick, boutique tasks that AI is now capable of doing within seconds. AI can build apps and websites, create graphic designs, write boilerplate legal text, create marketing, and even edit videos. These services are all becoming automated and highly commoditized, undermining the business models. In my view it’s a matter of time until people learn that they can recruit free AI to do these tasks for them, rather than paying freelancers to do them.

Tesla (TSLA), Palantir (PLTR): My thesis doesn’t rule out self-driving cars or advanced robotics, but both companies have extreme valuations reliant on tremendous AI progress. These companies would likely be major casualties of a burst AI bubble because they have such premium valuations. With Tesla in particular, robotaxis and humanoid robotics both require handling of novel edge cases. My framework of AI as a primarily interpolative rather than extrapolative engine would require edge cases to be close enough to something else that exists in the training set, so that AI could solve edge cases using rules it already knows. If the solution requires extrapolation, then AI is likely to fail. For Palantir, the problem is purely valuation. The business model is compatible with an "AI Centaur" model, but at 137x forward P/E, it's being priced more like a dominant, winner-take-all platform rather than a slightly differentiated competitor in a commodity market.

Nvidia (NVDA), AMD, Micron (MU): These companies are major infrastructure builders, but there’s a potential CapEx trap here. If I’m right and LLMs plateau and massive CapEx starts looking like throwing good money after bad, then the premiums these companies currently charge for their products can evaporate as demand dries up. Nvidia’s CUDA moat may keep it protected, while AMD and Micron, with more expensive relative valuations, are riskier bets in my view. Micron in particular is reminiscent of the steel mills of the 1880s - spending billions to expand capacity to meet today's demands, which might fizzle out within a few years. Micron's gross margins are currently more than double their 5Y average. When the memory boom ends, the company is vulnerable to a substantial margin contraction.

Lumentum (LITE): Lumentum is a “picks and shovels” AGI play. The problem with this company is that the replacement cycle for optical transceivers is a fraction of the initial build-out demand. When the initial build out is complete and Lumentum reverts to an upgrade/replacement cycle, then the major source of revenue could significantly diminish. Optical infrastructure is mostly CapEx, not OpEx, so LITE's recurrent revenue from upgrades is likely much less than its revenue from initial infrastructural expansion. The company is up 1000% in the last year with a P/E ratio near 100x, so it seems to me the company is being priced for continual CapEx. I certainly wouldn’t sell or short the stock right when it’s being inducted into the S&P 500 index, but I do think this company will go through a significant pullback in the short to medium term.

Conclusion

To recap: first, the quest for artificial general intelligence is premised on false mind-computer equivalence analogies. Although AI systems can do some incredible things, they will remain dark on the inside since they aren't built on the physical substrate required to produce conscious intelligence. This means that there will remain work and tasks where human cognition provides a significant advantage over the “philosophical zombies” that modern LLMs and AI agents are. That puts true AGI out of reach because AI will not be able to surpass the “human-AI centaur” in all domains. The “general” in “artificial general intelligence” is currently unattainable - humans utilizing AI tools are the “general” solution.

For investments, this entails that we’re spending trillions of dollars in pursuit of an unreachable goal. These trillions of dollars in AI infrastructure buildout are chasing a diminishing return on investment. Scaling data centers to be more massive doesn’t lead to machine cognition; it just creates slightly better versions of what we already have. That means that companies whose valuations are premised on CapEx spending continuing at current levels are in trouble, while companies that can capture efficiencies enabled by current AI tools, and that can successfully integrate AI to improve organizational productivity, are likely to be the big winners. Moreover, this pattern matches what we’ve observed in prior technological paradigm shifts.

Ultimately, I’m making the case that we shouldn’t sell ourselves short. The human brain is a vastly more remarkable object than any computer or data center. That likely means that we’ll still be required for quite a long time as conductors and facilitators of these tools. After all, they’re only repeating the things we’ve already discovered. The great utility of LLMs is that they give us quick, seamless access to the corpus of human-created knowledge.

Thanks for reading, and let me know in the comments which companies you think are big winners and losers within this thesis!

data.title: Market Brief: TAO And FET, Decentralized AI Breakout
data.description:
Bittensor (TAO-USD) and Artificial Superintelligence Alliance (FET-USD) posted two of the strongest rallies in crypto in March, driven by a convergence of catalysts that were specific to each protocol and to the decentralized AI sector as a whole.

For TAO, the immediate trigger was Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang's public endorsement of decentralized AI training on the All-in Podcast, a direct reference to Bittensor's architecture.

For FET, the driver was a rotation of capital into AI agent infrastructure as agentic AI moved from research to production across Anthropic, Google, and OpenAI. FET benefits from this narrative as the native token of ASI Chain, the platform designed to deploy and coordinate autonomous AI agents.

Project Primers: What are TAO and FET?

Bittensor (TAO) is a decentralized machine learning network that operates as a peer-to-peer marketplace for AI compute and model training. Participants, miners and validators, compete across specialized subnetworks called subnets, each focused on a distinct AI task such as text generation, inference, or compute routing.

Validators score miner outputs using Yuma Consensus, and TAO emissions are distributed to subnets in proportion to their real performance as determined by market-based staking flows.

The Artificial Superintelligence Alliance (FET) is the unified token of a three-way merger between Fetch.ai, SingularityNET, and CUDOS, forming a decentralized AI agent ecosystem.

FET is the native token of ASI:Chain, a blockDAG-based layer-1 optimized for high-concurrency AI workloads, and powers ASI:Create, a platform for building and deploying autonomous AI agents.

The alliance's roadmap targets an open beta for ASI:Create, a public TestNet, and a full mainnet launch by late 2026 or early 2027, at which point FET will be rebranded to ASI.

Bittensor (TAO)

ASI Alliance (FET)

Core function

Decentralized Machine Learning marketplace; subnets compete for compute rewards

Decentralized AI agent ecosystem

Architecture

Peer-to-peer subnets with Dynamic TAO (dTAO) AMM per subnet

ASI:Chain blockDAG L1 + ASI:Create agent deployment platform

Key milestone

Covenant 72B: largest permissionless LLM pre-train on record (arXiv, Mar 2026)

ASI:Chain DevNet beta live; mainnet targeted late 2026/early 2027

Halving/Burn Mechanism

Dec 2025: daily emissions cut from 7,200 → 3,600 TAO

$50M Earn & Burn mechanism; deflationary via platform fee buybacks

TAO: Fundamental Strength vs. Technical Overextension

TAO's March rally is harder to dismiss as purely speculative because the on-chain data has moved first. Subnet staking grew from approximately $74,000 one year ago to over $620 million, with active subnets expanding from 80 to over 120. Approximately 75% of circulating TAO supply is staked, compressing available float.

The December 2025 halving cut daily emissions from 7,200 to 3,600 TAO, reducing annual inflation from the mid-20% range to roughly 13%. With 75% of supply now staked and daily supply compressed, the float available on exchanges is structurally thin.

Technical signals, however, are strained. Daily RSI reached 74–76 by March 25, in overbought territory. Futures open interest tripled from $130M in early March to over $500M now in under two weeks.

Source:Coinglass

FET: Less Extended than TAO, but Still Overheated

The fundamental backdrop provides partial justification for the move. ASI:Chain DevNet beta is live. ASI:Create closed alpha launched in February 2026. The roadmap targets open beta and TestNet in 2026, with mainnet by late 2026 or early 2027.

A $50M Earn & Burn mechanism, where platform service fees buy and burn FET, introduces a deflationary supply mechanic as adoption grows. The pending migration to a unified ASI token adds a structural consolidation narrative.

For on-chain metrics, exchange reserves fell to a 2024 low, a bullish read. When tokens move off exchanges onto private wallets, it means holders are withdrawing rather than positioning to sell. And the increase in open interest is less extreme compared to TAO.

Source: cryptoquant

Source: Coinglass

Bottom Line

Both tokens have been repriced aggressively, with TAO recording an RSI of 76 and futures OI tripled in under two weeks; the risk profile is elevated. FET's movement is less extreme in magnitude, though short-term sentiment has overheated as well.

The structural thesis that capital is rotating into verifiable decentralized AI infrastructure holds for both, but much of the near-term catalyst is probably already priced in. Risk is accumulating faster than fundamental progress.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.
